title: Array Superpowers 🦸
description: Add, remove, and manipulate data using built-in array methods.

slides.title: It's an Object! 💎
slides.content: Since EVERYTHING in Ruby is an object, Arrays have incredibly powerful built-in methods you can attach to them using a dot `.`.

slides.title: Adding Data (Push) 📥
slides.content:
To add a brand new item to the very END of your array list, use the `.push` method.

```ruby
nums = [1, 2, 3]
nums.push(4)
# nums is now [1, 2, 3, 4]
```

*Ruby shorthand:* You can also use the "shovel" operator `<<` to do the exact same thing!
`nums << 5`

slides.title: Push vs Shovel
slides.content: Both are doing the exact same operation: shoving a new piece of data to the back of the list.

quizData.question: What does the shovel operator `<<` do to an Array?

options.text: Adds the item to the very beginning of the Array.
Adds the item to the very end of the Array.

quizData.explanation: It's identical to `.push`. It shovels new data to the back end.

slides.title: Removing Data (Pop) 📤
slides.content:
To permanently remove and return the very LAST item of an Array, use the `.pop` method.

```ruby
stack = ["Plate 1", "Plate 2", "Plate 3"]
top_plate = stack.pop

# top_plate is now "Plate 3"
# stack is now ["Plate 1", "Plate 2"]
```

quizData.question: Which method permanently rips the final item out of the array?
quizData.explanation: `.pop` reaches in, grabs the exact last item, permanently removes it from the array, and hands it to you.

slides.title: Bonus Methods 🎁
slides.content:
There are so many! Try writing these out on your own:
* `.length` (Count the items)
* `.reverse` (Flip it backward)
* `.sort` (Put it in alphabetical/numerical order)
